%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.4-3 The Exponential Function \(e^{st}\)

> **Section Objective:** This section explains how the complex exponential \(e^{st}\) unifies constants, exponentials, sinusoids, and growing or decaying sinusoids into a single signal model.

---

**Concepts In This Section**

- Complex frequency
- Expansion of \(e^{st}\)
- Growth/decay versus oscillation
- s-plane
- Conjugate exponential representation

## 1. Complex frequency

**Complex frequency** \(s\) is a single symbol that encodes two independent signal behaviors. The real part \(\sigma\) (sigma) controls whether the signal's amplitude grows, stays constant, or decays over time. The imaginary-axis coefficient \(\omega\) (omega) controls how fast the signal oscillates. The symbol \(j\) marks the imaginary axis, defined by \(j^2 = -1\).

**Minimal example:** For \(s = 2 + j5\), we have \(\sigma = 2\) and \(\omega = 5\). The positive \(\sigma\) means the amplitude grows; the nonzero \(\omega\) means the signal oscillates.

#### Exam Trigger
When a signal has both an exponential envelope and a sinusoidal oscillation, look for \(s = \sigma + j\omega\).

#### COMMON MISUSE
\(\omega\) is the real-valued radian frequency — it is the coefficient of \(j\), not the full term \(j\omega\). Do not treat \(\omega\) and \(j\omega\) as interchangeable.

$$s = \sigma + j\omega$$

## 2. Expanding the complex exponential

**Eq. (1.13)** is the core formula of this section. It splits \(e^{st}\) into two parts:

- **Amplitude envelope:** \(e^{\sigma t}\) — grows if \(\sigma > 0\), stays at 1 if \(\sigma = 0\), decays if \(\sigma < 0\).
- **Rotating sinusoidal part:** \(\cos\omega t + j\sin\omega t\) — the real cosine and imaginary sine components that oscillate at rate \(\omega\).

Symbol guide: \(t\) is time, \(\sigma\) sets the growth or decay rate, \(\omega\) sets the oscillation rate, and \(j\) marks the quadrature (imaginary) component.

**Representative example:**

$$e^{(2+j5)t} = e^{2t}(\cos 5t + j\sin 5t)$$

This is a growing oscillatory complex exponential — the \(e^{2t}\) envelope expands while \(\cos 5t + j\sin 5t\) rotates.

#### Exam Trigger
Expand using Eq. (1.13) whenever asked to classify signal behavior from a given \(s\).

#### COMMON MISUSE
\(e^{st}\) is sinusoidal only when \(\sigma = 0\). When \(\sigma \neq 0\), the amplitude is not constant.

$$e^{st} = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)t} = e^{\sigma t}(\cos\omega t + j\sin\omega t)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.21](/figures/page-090-figure_1_21-1.png)
*Figure 1.21 shows how different choices of \(\sigma\) and \(\omega\) produce constants, exponentials, sinusoids, decaying sinusoids, and growing sinusoids.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains four panels, each plotting a waveform versus time \(t\). Panel (a) shows pure real exponentials \(e^{\sigma t}\) for \(\sigma &gt; 0\) (growth), \(\sigma = 0\) (flat constant line), and \(\sigma &lt; 0\) (decay), with no oscillation since \(\omega = 0\). Panel (b) shows an undamped sinusoid with \(\sigma = 0\), where the amplitude envelope is flat and the waveform oscillates at constant amplitude. Panels (c) and (d) show sinusoids bounded by dashed exponential envelope curves: panel (c) has \(\sigma &lt; 0\) so the envelope shrinks and the oscillation fades, while panel (d) has \(\sigma &gt; 0\) so the envelope expands and the oscillation grows. Students should notice that \(\sigma\) alone controls whether the envelope decays, stays constant, or grows, while nonzero \(\omega\) introduces sinusoidal variation.</div>

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="parameter_effect_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Real sinusoids from conjugate exponentials

**Eq. (1.14)** shows how to write a real exponentially varying cosine as the average of two conjugate complex exponentials.

Here \(s = \sigma + j\omega\) and its conjugate is \(s^* = \sigma - j\omega\). When you expand both exponentials using Eq. (1.13), the imaginary sine parts are equal and opposite, so they cancel. What remains is twice the real cosine part, and dividing by 2 gives the cosine alone.

**Representative example:**

$$e^{2t}\cos 5t = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{(2+j5)t} + e^{(2-j5)t}\right)$$

The two frequencies \(2 + j5\) and \(2 - j5\) are a conjugate pair in the right half-plane.

#### Exam Trigger
Use Eq. (1.14) whenever a cosine with an exponential envelope must be rewritten in complex-exponential form.

#### COMMON MISUSE
- Forgetting the factor \(\frac{1}{2}\) — the pair must be averaged, not just summed.
- Using only one complex exponential — a single \(e^{st}\) includes an imaginary sine part that does not cancel.

$$e^{\sigma t}\cos\omega t = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{st} + e^{s^{*}t}\right)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.22](/figures/page-091-figure_1_22-1.png)
*Figure 1.22 shows the s-plane, where the sign of \(\sigma\) separates decaying signals (left half-plane) from growing signals (right half-plane).*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure displays the complex frequency plane (s-plane) with a horizontal real axis labeled \(\sigma\) and a vertical imaginary axis labeled \(j\omega\). The plane is divided into a left half-plane (\(\sigma &lt; 0\)) and a right half-plane (\(\sigma &gt; 0\)), with annotations indicating that points in the left half-plane correspond to exponentially decreasing signals and points in the right half-plane correspond to exponentially increasing signals. Points lying exactly on the imaginary axis have \(\sigma = 0\), meaning the signal amplitude stays constant. Students should notice that the real part \(\sigma\) alone determines decay versus growth, while the imaginary coordinate \(j\omega\) determines the oscillation frequency.</div>

## 4. Special Cases and Exam Classification

Every signal of the form \(e^{st}\) falls into one of these categories. Classify by \(\sigma\) first, then by \(\omega\).

| Condition | Signal type |
|---|---|
| \(s = 0\) | Constant dc signal |
| \(\omega = 0,\; \sigma \neq 0\) | Monotonic exponential \(e^{\sigma t}\) |
| \(\sigma = 0,\; \omega \neq 0\) | Constant-amplitude sinusoid |
| \(\sigma < 0,\; \omega \neq 0\) | Exponentially decaying sinusoid |
| \(\sigma > 0,\; \omega \neq 0\) | Exponentially growing sinusoid |
| \(\sigma < 0,\; \omega = 0\) | Monotonic exponential decay |
| \(\sigma > 0,\; \omega = 0\) | Monotonic exponential growth |

**Worked classification example:**

The signal \(e^{-2t}\cos 5t\) has complex frequencies \(s = -2 \pm j5\). Both points lie in the **left half-plane** (\(\sigma = -2 < 0\)), so the signal **decays**. Since \(\omega = 5 \neq 0\), the signal also **oscillates**. Classification: exponentially decaying sinusoid.

> **Exam Note:** Always classify by the sign of \(\sigma\) first, then check whether \(\omega\) is zero or nonzero.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Complex frequency is defined as \(s = \sigma + j\omega\), where \(\sigma\) controls the amplitude envelope and \(\omega\) controls oscillation rate.
- The core expansion (Eq. 1.13): \(e^{st} = e^{\sigma t}(\cos\omega t + j\sin\omega t)\) — envelope times rotating sinusoid.
- Real cosine representation (Eq. 1.14): \(e^{\sigma t}\cos\omega t = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{st} + e^{s^{*}t}\right)\) — always use the conjugate pair with factor \(\frac{1}{2}\).
- s-plane rule: \(\sigma < 0\) means exponential decay (LHP), \(\sigma = 0\) means constant amplitude (imaginary axis), \(\sigma > 0\) means exponential growth (RHP).
- Classify any signal by checking \(\sigma\) sign first, then whether \(\omega = 0\) (no oscillation) or \(\omega \neq 0\) (oscillation present).

*Next, we will use these signal models to analyze signals and systems more efficiently.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
